diagrams.title: Error Control Mechanism in Data Link Layer
diagrams.description: Shows the complete error control process from sender to receiver including error detection, feedback, and retransmission
elements: Sender Node - contains data to transmit
Framing Unit - adds frame headers and trailers with error detection codes
Noisy Channel - represented as a path with lightning symbols indicating potential noise interference
Received Frame - arrives at receiver with possible bit errors
Error Detection Unit - checks for errors using CRC/Parity/Chessum
Decision Diamond - YES/NO branch for error detection
If Error: NACK frame sent back to sender with negative acknowledgment
If No Error: ACK frame sent back to sender with positive acknowledgment
Retransmission Controller - initiates retransmission upon receiving NACK or timeout
Arrow showing retransmission path back to sender
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the complete flow of error control including how errors are detected at receiver and how feedback (ACK/NACK) triggers retransmission at sender

diagrams.title: Types of Data Errors - Single Bit vs Burst Error
diagrams.description: Visual comparison of single-bit errors and burst errors in transmitted data frames
elements: Frame A with Single-Bit Error: Shows a row of 8 bits (0 1 1 0 1 0 0 1) with ONE bit flipped from 0 to 1 at position 4, highlighted in red
Annotation: 'Single-bit error - only 1 bit affected, easier to detect and correct'
Frame B with Burst Error: Shows consecutive bits (positions 3-7) flipped/changed, highlighted in red as a continuous block
Annotation: 'Burst error - multiple consecutive bits affected, more challenging to handle'
Burst length indicator showing the gap between first and last error bit
Channel noise sources represented: EMI, interference, attenuation symbols
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the fundamental difference between single-bit and burst errors, recognize that burst errors are more common in data communication, and learn why error detection/correction methods must handle both types

diagrams.title: Stop-and-Wait ARQ Protocol
diagrams.description: Shows the complete timing diagram of Stop-and-Wait ARQ with successful transmission and retransmission scenarios
elements: Time axis (horizontal) showing sequential events
Sender: Sends Frame 0, waits
Arrow down to channel showing Frame 0 transmission
Receiver: Receives Frame 0, detects no error, sends ACK 0
Arrow up showing ACK 0 returning
Sender: Receives ACK 0, sends Frame 1 (next frame)
Timeout representation: If ACK not received within timeout period, show automatic retransmission of Frame 0
Lost Frame scenario: Show Frame 1 lost in transit, receiver times out waiting, sender retransmits after timeout
Duplicate Frame scenario: Show receiver receiving duplicate frame and discarding it
Sequence numbers clearly labeled (0, 1, 0, 1)
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the operation of Stop-and-Wait ARQ, understand the use of sequence numbers, timeout mechanism, and how duplicate frames are handled

diagrams.title: Sliding Window Protocols - Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat
diagrams.description: Comparative diagram showing window size and retransmission behavior for Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat ARQ
elements: Go-Back-N Window: Sender window showing N=4, frames 0-3 in window, frames 4-7 outside
Receiver window showing only expected frame (e.g., expecting 3)
Retransmission arrow showing ALL frames from error point being resent
Selective Repeat Window: Sender window showing N=4 with individual frame statuses (sent, acknowledged, pending)
Receiver window showing buffering of out-of-order frames
Selective retransmission showing ONLY the erroneous frame being resent
Sequence number circles for both protocols
ACK/NAK annotations for each
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the key difference between Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat protocols, particularly how Go-Back-N retransmits all subsequent frames while Selective Repeat retransmits only the lost frame, and how each handles out-of-order delivery

charts.title: Comparison of ARQ Protocols
charts.content: Columns: Protocol Name, Window Size, Retransmission Strategy, Acknowledgment Type, Efficiency, Use Cases. Rows: Stop-and-Wait (Window=1, retransmits single frame, ACK for each frame, simple but inefficient, LANs), Go-Back-N (Window=N, retransmits all frames from error, cumulative ACK, moderate efficiency, moderate speed networks), Selective Repeat (Window≤N/2, retransmits only error frame, selective ACK, highest efficiency, high-speed networks)

charts.title: Error Detection and Handling Process
charts.content: Start → Is data received? → No: Wait for timeout → Timeout: Retransmit frame. Yes → Perform error detection (Parity/CRC/Checksum) → Error detected? → No: Deliver data to upper layer, Send ACK. Yes: Discard frame, Send NACK → Return to wait state. Each decision point shows YES/NO branches clearly.

charts.title: Error Control Techniques Classification
charts.content: Main branch: Error Control. Level 2: Error Detection (Parity Check - Simple/VRC, Checksum, CRC) and Error Correction (Hamming Code - Single-bit correction, Burst error correction). Level 3 for ARQ: Stop-and-Wait ARQ, Go-Back-N ARQ, Selective Repeat ARQ. Each technique shows brief purpose description.

charts.title: Error Detection Methods Comparison
charts.content: Columns: Method, Mechanism, Detection Capability, Overhead, Complexity. Rows: Simple Parity (Even/Odd parity bit, single-bit errors, 1 bit, Low), Two-Dimensional Parity (Matrix parity, single and some burst errors, 2 bits, Low), Checksum (Complement addition, burst errors up to word length, Log(n) bits, Medium), CRC (Polynomial division, burst errors up to degree, r+1 bits, Medium-High).

mindMaps.centralConcept: Error Control in Data Link Layer
branches: Error Detection (Parity Check, Checksum, CRC) - Adds redundant bits to detect errors
Error Correction (Forward Error Correction using Hamming Code) - Corrects errors without retransmission
ARQ Protocols (Stop-and-Wait, Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat) - Automatic Repeat reQuest for reliability
Flow Control (Sliding Window) - Manages sender rate to prevent receiver overflow
Connection-Oriented vs Connectionless - Reliability levels in data link services
Framing - Organizes data into manageable units for error control
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting all error control concepts, showing relationships between detection, correction, and retransmission mechanisms, and how they fit into the broader data link layer services for reliable communication
